answer:
**In Allama Iqbal's poem "Sarguzasht-e-Adam" ("The Saga of Man") from *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* (chapter 37), Adam narrates his own "fall" not as a mere sin of disobedience but as a deliberate embrace of restless consciousness and an eternal quest for cosmic truth, which shattered the peace of Paradise and launched humanity's dynamic journey through history.[^1][^2]**

Iqbal reimagines the Biblical/Quranic expulsion from Eden as Adam's voluntary choice: **"My spirit found no rest in Paradise’s domain, / Once the fiery cup of consciousness I chose to drain."**[^3] This "fiery cup" symbolizes awakening to self-awareness and change-loving nature, contrasting Paradise's static bliss. Adam introduces his tale as **"the story of my exile’s way; / The tale of the First Covenant I let fall away,"**[^4] framing the fall as rejecting divine complacency ('First Covenant') for adventure.

From there, Adam recounts his odyssey—embodying prophets (Moses on Sinai, Jesus on the Cross, Muhammad in Hira), wandering nations (India, Greece, China), sparking wars of faith vs. reason, and pioneering science (gravity, lightning)—all without ultimate satisfaction until realizing **"in my own heart’s house He did reside."**[^5] Iqbal thus portrays the fall as humanity's ennobling origin: a heroic rupture from inertia toward self-discovery, divine intimacy, and creative strife.[^6]

[^1]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 37 (*Sarguzasht-e-Adam*), [full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/).

[^2]: Poem opens: "[p-4b8f05] The Saga of Man," from [passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/#p-4b8f05).

[^3]: [p-3d6971], from [passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/#p-3d6971).

[^4]: [p-7c0f77], from [passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/#p-7c0f77).

[^5]: [p-08cb93], from [passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/37-sarguzasht-e-adam/translation/#p-08cb93).

[^6]: The poem spans 19 verses tracing this arc; see full text via [^1].
